results.title: On the Justification of Legal Norms
results.fullText: This article defends a claim about how legal norms come to be justified: that justification is neither a matter of bare enactment nor of moral correctness alone, but of a reasoned relationship between the two that institutions are built to sustain. I proceed in three steps. First I set out the positivist intuition that a norm's validity depends on its source rather than its merits, and I grant what is right in it. Second I press the objection that source alone cannot explain why officials treat some valid norms as defective, and I argue that this practice reveals a justificatory dimension the pure source theory cannot accommodate. Third I propose an account on which justification is a standing demand that a norm's content answer to reasons the affected parties could accept, mediated by the institutions that apply it. The positivist is correct that we identify law by its sources; a rule enacted through the recognized procedure is law whatever we think of it. But identification is not justification. When a judge describes a valid statute as one that ought to be amended, the judge is not confusing law with morality; the judge is invoking a criterion internal to legal practice itself, namely that the law hold itself answerable to the reasons it purports to serve. A theory that cannot represent this criterion mistakes a feature of the practice for a confusion about it. The account I propose treats justification as gradient rather than binary. A norm is more or less justified to the degree that its content can be defended to those it binds by reasons they have standing to weigh, and to the degree that the institutions applying it are structured to test that defense rather than to foreclose it. This explains why procedural fairness and substantive merit are not rival theories of legitimacy but two aspects of a single demand. I close by considering the objection that this account collapses into a thin proceduralism. It does not, because the reasons that justify a norm are not exhausted by the process that produced it; a fair process can still yield a norm whose content fails the justificatory test, and the practice of internal critique I have described is precisely the mechanism by which that failure is registered and, over time, corrected.
